coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots induce the construction of a sulfur vacancy-rich CaIn2S4/NGQDs photocatalyst with a thin layer for achieving efficient photocatalytic degradation

coredata.dc:description: The loading of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can significantly enhance the photocatalytic capability of ternary metal chalcogenides catalysts. In this study, a CaIn2S4/NGQDs photocatalyst was synthesized through a two-step hydrothermal method, achieving high-efficiency photocatalytic degradation performance. Under simulated sunlight irradiation (300 W Xenon lamp) for 40 minutes, the degradation rate of rhodamine B (RhB, 30 mg/L) by CaIn2S4/NGQDs-2 reached 99.8 %, with a reaction kinetic constant of −0.1254 min−1, which is 6.2 times higher than that of pure CaIn2S4. Furthermore, CaIn2S4/NGQDs exhibited excellent photocatalytic stability. The superior photocatalytic performance of CaIn2S4/NGQDs-2 can be attributed to the role of NGQDs as a surfactant, resulting in a thin lamellar structure of CaIn2S4, which not only shortens the migration path of photogenerated carriers but also increases the specific surface area of CaIn2S4/NGQDs-2. The increase in the number of sulfur vacancies (Sv) captures more excited electrons to effectively suppress the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Meanwhile, NGQDs as an electron acceptor, a directional electron transfer channel was established to enhance the electron transfer path between Sv-CaIn2S4 and NGQDs. In conclusion, this study presented a novel strategy for enhancing the photocatalytic performance of CaIn2S4 for water treatment applications.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots induce the construction of a sulfur vacancy-rich CaIn2S4/NGQDs photocatalyst with a thin layer for achieving efficient photocatalytic degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The loading of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can significantly enhance the photocatalytic capability of ternary metal chalcogenides catalysts. In this study, a CaIn2S4/NGQDs photocatalyst was synthesized through a two-step hydrothermal method, achieving high-efficiency photocatalytic degradation performance. Under simulated sunlight irradiation (300 W Xenon lamp) for 40 minutes, the degradation rate of rhodamine B (RhB, 30 mg/L) by CaIn2S4/NGQDs-2 reached 99.8 %, with a reaction kinetic constant of −0.1254 min−1, which is 6.2 times higher than that of pure CaIn2S4. Furthermore, CaIn2S4/NGQDs exhibited excellent photocatalytic stability. The superior photocatalytic performance of CaIn2S4/NGQDs-2 can be attributed to the role of NGQDs as a surfactant, resulting in a thin lamellar structure of CaIn2S4, which not only shortens the migration path of photogenerated carriers but also increases the specific surface area of CaIn2S4/NGQDs-2. The increase in the number of sulfur vacancies (Sv) captures more excited electrons to effectively suppress the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Meanwhile, NGQDs as an electron acceptor, a directional electron transfer channel was established to enhance the electron transfer path between Sv-CaIn2S4 and NGQDs. In conclusion, this study presented a novel strategy for enhancing the photocatalytic performance of CaIn2S4 for water treatment applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the acceleration of globalization, the issues of environmental pollution and energy scarcity have become increasingly prominent, posing urgent challenges that need to be addressed [1]. Against this backdrop, photocatalysis technology emerges as an environmentally friendly solution, whose significance cannot be underestimated. Within the realm of photocatalysis, selecting the appropriate photocatalyst is of utmost importance [2]. In recent years, the research on ZnIn2S4 has gained enormous attention, sparking interest in ternary metal sulfides [3]. In earlier studies, ternary indium-based sulfides were generally recognized for their unique photoelectric and catalytic properties [4]. Among ternary indium-based sulfide semiconductor photocatalysts, CaIn2S4 stands out as a low-toxicity, non-noble metal material that offers the advantages of being environmentally friendly and cost-effective [5]. Possessing a cubic symmetric crystal structure and a narrow bandgap of 1.9 eV, CaIn2S4 extends its light absorption range to 600 nm [6]. Consequently, CaIn2S4 emerged as a suitable candidate for photocatalysis research, attributable to its outstanding visible light absorption capabilities, suitable conduction, and valence band positions [7]. However, pure-phase CaIn2S4 photocatalysts also have limitations such as rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and low separation efficiency, which restrict their photocatalysis application [8]. To enhance the application potential of CaIn2S4 in photocatalysis, researchers often modify it through methods such as carbon-based material loading [9], element doping [10], interlayer engineering [11], and vacancy engineering [12].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), with a size less than 10 nanometers, exhibit non-toxic, hydrophilic, and electrical properties, making them an ideal material for photocatalyzing organic pollutants [13]. Nitrogen doping, due to its similar ionic radius to carbon, is commonly used to enhance GQDs' functionality. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), benefiting from the presence of abundant ionic groups such as amino groups, exhibit superior light absorption capacity, conductivity, and more edge active sites compared to undoped GQDs [14]. Additionally, the inherent conjugated π-structure of NGQDs provides superior electron storage and transport capabilities for the catalyst. For instance, M. Cynthia Goh et al. synthesized titanium dioxide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (TiO2/NGQDs) photocatalysts with different NGQDs contents [15]. The loading of NGQDs effectively enhanced the visible light absorbance of the 8 wt% TiO2/NGQDs composite, resulting in a phenol degradation efficiency of 613 % compared to P25 TiO2 under visible light irradiation.

Among two-dimensional (2D) materials, interlayer engineering, which facilitates the exposure of more active sites and enables easier charge transport, is considered one of the effective approaches to enhance catalytic performance [16]. For instance, Peng Chen et al. fabricated a GQD/Co-MoS2 photocatalyst with an interlayer spacing of 9.7 Å, which exhibited significantly superior performance compared to the Co-MoS2 without GQD addition [17]. The performance improvement can be attributed to the enlarged interlayer spacing, which not only promotes charge transfer efficiency at the GQD/MoS2 0D/2D van der Waals heterojunction (vdWH), but also reduces the bandgap width. Vacancy engineering is also recognized as an effective strategy to enhance photocatalytic performance [18]. It can enhance the absorption of visible light by reducing the bandgap width, capture more photogenerated electrons, thereby reducing the recombination of charge carriers, and ultimately improve the overall photocatalytic efficiency. For example, Enzhou Liu et al. prepared a GQD/TiO2 material [19]. The introduction of defect energy levels through oxygen vacancies enabled electrons to be easily transported through hydrogen bonds, which in turn led to more efficient carrier separation under visible light irradiation and enhanced activity in the photocatalytic simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and RhB.

In this study, the CaIn2S4/NGQDs photocatalyst was successfully synthesized using a two-step hydrothermal method and revealed the dual functionality of NGQDs. On one hand, NGQDs act as surfactants to construct the lamellar structure of CaIn2S4. On the other hand, as a carbon material, NGQDs facilitate charge transfer between CaIn2S4 and NGQDs, enabling efficient degradation of RhB. Furthermore, the photocatalytic reaction mechanism of the CaIn2S4/NGQDs photocatalyst to provide a new design perspective for investigating the performance of carbon-based quantum dot-modified CaIn2S4 photocatalysts.

The following chemicals were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. and are of analytical reagent grade, requiring no further purification: Calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2·2 H2O), Indium trichloride, tetrahydrate (InCl3·4 H2O), Thioacetamide (C2H5NS), Anhydrous citric acid (C6H8O7), Urea (CH4N2O), Rhodamine B (C28H31ClN2O3), Methylene blue (C16H18ClN3S), Congo red (C32H22N6Na2O6S2), Methyl orange (C14H14N3NaO3S), Tetracycline (C22H24N2O8), Levofloxacin (C18H20FN3O4), Anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH), p-Benzoquinone (C6H4O2), Tert-butanol (C4H10O), Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (C10H14N2Na2O8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare NGQDs, 5 mmol of anhydrous citric acid and 15 mmol of urea (in a ratio of 1:3) were sequentially added to 25 mL of deionized water [20]. After thorough mixing for 30 minutes to ensure homogeneity, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Subsequently, the autoclave was placed in an environment maintained at 180°C for 6 hours. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 minutes, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain an aqueous solution of NGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CaIn2S4 and CaIn2S4/NGQDs photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Different amounts of NGQDs aqueous solutions (0 mL, 5 mL, 10 mL, 15 mL), 1 mmol of calcium chloride dihydrate, 2 mmol of indium trichloride tetrahydrate, and 6 mmol of thioacetamide [21] were sequentially added to (60 mL, 55 mL, 50 mL, 45 mL) of deionized water, respectively. The mixture was stirred for 60 minutes to ensure complete dissolution. Subsequently, the suspension was carefully transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was then placed in a forced-air drying oven maintained at 160 °C for 12 hours. After naturally cooling to ambient temperature, the reaction product was washed three times with deionized water and ethanol, respectively, and the resulting precipitate was collected by centrifugation. The collected solid was finally dried at 70 °C for 12 hours and ground to obtain the final product. The products corresponding to the NGQDs aqueous solution dosages of 0 mL, 5 mL, 10 mL, and 15 mL were labeled as CaIn2S4, CaIS/NGQDs-1, CaIS/NGQDs-2, and CaIS/NGQDs-3, respectively.

The crystal structures of the samples were characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance). The infrared spectra were analyzed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific Nicolette, USA). The elemental composition of the samples was analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI). The defects of the sample were determined using Electron Spin Resonance (ESR, A300–10/12, Bruker, Germany). The morphological characteristics of the samples were examined utilizing a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Model SU8020). Energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS) and elemental mapping were conducted concurrently with the SEM instrumentation. To assess the detailed microstructure of the samples, transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30) was employed. The surface roughness of the sample was measured using Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon, Germany). The specific surface area and pore volume of the nanomaterials were precisely determined using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller method (BET, Micromeritics ASAP 2460, USA). The photoluminescent properties of the samples were measured using photoluminescent spectra (PL, Edinburgh FLS1000, UK). Analysis of the diffuse reflectance spectra was performed using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Hitachi U-3010). Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and photocurrent response curves were acquired using an electrochemical workstation (CHI-760E).

Taking RhB as an example, a photocatalyst (10 mg) was added to a 50 mL solution of RhB prepared in ultrapure water at a concentration of 30 mg/L in a beaker. The solution was then stirred at a speed of 500 rpm in a dark environment for 30 minutes to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium. After this, stirring continued at 500 rpm while the solution was irradiated with a 300 W Xenon lamp light source (PLS-SXE300 +/UV, AM 1.5 G, with the beaker placed approximately 12 cm below the xenon lamp). Subsequently, every 10 minutes, 4 mL of the degradation solution was taken from the reaction liquid and centrifuged at high speed to retain the supernatant. The absorbance of the RhB degradation products at different times was measured using an Ultraviolet-Visible Spectrophotometer (UV–vis). The formula for calculating the degradation rate and first-order degradation kinetics of the photocatalyst is as follows:

In the formula, C0 represents the original absorbance of RhB, Ct indicates the absorbance of the solution after photocatalytic degradation at different times, C1 denotes the absorbance after dark adsorption, K (min−1) is the reaction rate constant, and t (min) is the reaction time.

Fig. 1(a) presents the XRD patterns of CaIn2S4, CaIS/NGQDs-1, CaIS/NGQDs-2, and CaIS/NGQDs-3. The initial peaks of CaIn2S4 at 2θ = 27.3°, 33.2°, and 47.6° correspond to the (311), (400), and (440) crystal planes of the cubic structure of CaIn2S4 (PDF#31–0272), respectively [22]. The decrease in peak intensities observed for CaIS/NGQDs-X (X = 1, 2, 3) can be attributed to the suppressive effect of NGQDs loaded on the CaIn2S4, which subsequently leads to a reduction in the crystallinity of all composite samples [23]. Furthermore, the slight shift of the peak positions towards higher angles may be attributed to the occurrence of sulfur vacancies (Sv) [24].

The surface functional groups characterizing CaIn2S4, CaIS/NGQDs-1, CaIS/NGQDs-2, and CaIS/NGQDs-3 are presented in Fig. 1(b) of the FT-IR spectra. In CaIn2S4, the peaks at 3417 cm−1 and 1398 cm−1 correspond to hydroxyl groups (-OH) from surface-adsorbed water molecules [25]. Similarly, in CaIS/NGQDs-X (X = 1, 2, 3), a significantly enhanced peak for O-H stretching vibrations can be observed, along with absorption bands for CO and C-O stretching vibrations at around 1606 cm−1 and 1001 cm−1, respectively [26]. These observations suggest the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of CaIS/NGQDs-X (X = 1, 2, 3). Notably, characteristic peaks at 3195 cm−1 and 1144 cm−1, which are absent in CaIn2S4, are observed in CaIS/NGQDs-X (X = 1, 2, 3) [27]. These peaks, corresponding to N-H and C-N bonds respectively, serve as conclusive evidence for the successful introduction of nitrogen elements after the modification with NGQDs. These functional groups, including C-OH, CO, and N-H, can enhance electron transfer efficiency. The successful synthesis of CaIS/NGQDs composites is corroborated by the presence of all the characteristic peaks of NGQDs in their respective FT-IR spectra [28].

The surface elemental valence states of CaIn2S4 and CaIS/NGQDs-2 were deeply analyzed using XPS [29]. In the full-scan XPS spectrum of CaIS/NGQDs-2 ( Fig. 2(a)), the presence of O, In, N, Ca, C, and S elements was observed. Fig. 2(b) presents the Ca 2p spectrum, revealing that the binding energies of CaIS/NGQDs-2 at the Ca 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 double peaks of Ca2+ are 351.27 eV and 347.67 eV, respectively, both lower than those of CaIn2S4 (351.49 eV and 347.89 eV) [30]. Similarly, in the In 3d spectrum (Fig. 2(c)), the binding energies of CaIS/NGQDs-2 (452.28 eV and 444.75 eV) at the In 3d3/2 and In 3d5/2 orbitals of In3+ are also lower than those of CaIn2S4 (452.46 eV and 444.92 eV) [31]. Fig. 2(d) depicts the S 2p spectrum, where the S 2p1/2 and S 2p2/3 characteristic peaks of CaIS/NGQDs-2 shift towards lower binding energies at 162.46 eV and 161.24 eV, respectively, compared to CaIn2S4 (162.64 eV and 161.45 eV) [32]. These results indicate that the escape of S atoms from the catalyst leads to a significant number of S vacancies in CaIS/NGQDs-2, causing the S 2p spectrum to shift towards lower binding energies [33]. Additionally, the Ca and In ions adjacent to the S vacancies gain excess electron density near the abundant defects, further leading to the downward shift in the Ca 2p and In 3d spectra towards lower binding energies [34].

The C 1 s spectrum of CaIS/NGQDs-2 ( Fig. 3(a)) can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 288.78 eV, 285.91 eV, 284.91 eV, and 284.21 eV, corresponding to CO, CN, C-C, and CC bonds, respectively [35]. The N 1 s spectrum of CaIS/NGQDs-2 (Fig. 3(b)) exhibits three distinct types of nitrogen, namely graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N, with binding energies of 401.79 eV, 400.10 eV, and 399.37 eV, respectively [36]. Graphite-N can enhance the conductivity and hydrophilicity of the sample, while pyrrolic-N can provide a richer set of active sites and improve the electron-hole pair separation ability [37]. Consequently, CaIS/NGQDs-2 through the loading of NGQDs, effectively increases the number of active sites, conductivity, and electron-hole pair separation capability, thereby enhancing its photocatalytic degradation capacity for organic pollutants. The O 1 s spectrum of CaIS/NGQDs-2 (Fig. 3(c)) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 533.01 eV, 531.84 eV, and 530.66 eV, corresponding to C-OH, CO, and COOH groups, respectively [38]. These hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl functional groups facilitate the NGQDs dispersion in aqueous solvents, conferring excellent stability and hydrophilicity [39,40]. The effective compositing of NGQDs with CaIn₂S₄ was further confirmed by analyzing the XRD patterns, FT-IR spectra, and XPS spectra [41]. To further confirm the existence of S vacancies, room-temperature ESR measurements were conducted. As shown in Fig. 3(d), compared to CaIn2S4, CaIS/NGQDs-2 exhibits a significantly enhanced ESR signal at 3503 G (with a g-value of 2.005) [42]. Increasing g-value intensity is mainly ascribed to the more Sv may be fabricated by adsorption of NGQDs on the surface of CaIn2S4 layer during the hydrothermal process, which is consistent with the XPS results.

The morphology of CaIn2S4 and CaIS/NGQDs-2 was investigated using SEM [43]. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the large agglomerates of CaIn2S4 mostly exhibited diameters greater than 200 nm. However, in Fig. 4(b), the agglomerate diameter of CaIS/NGQDs-2 was significantly reduced. Combined with Fig. 4(c), it can be observed that CaIS/NGQDs-2 was primarily composed of nano-flower-like aggregates with an average diameter of approximately 50 nm. The reduced size of CaIS/NGQDs-2 is attributed to the significant hindrance of CaIn2S4 growth caused by the loading of NGQDs. To verify the successful loading of NGQDs on CaIn2S4, SEM-Mapping was used to analyze the elemental composition of CaIS/NGQDs-2. As shown in Fig. 4(d-i), the elements Ca, In, S, C, and N were evenly distributed on the surface of the sample [44].

The microstructure of CaIS/NGQDs-2 was further observed using AFM, TEM, and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). As shown in Fig. 5(a), CaIS/NGQDs-2 exhibits a thin-layer structure with a root mean square roughness of 0.37 nm measured by AFM [45]. Additionally, Fig. 5(b, c) demonstrate that NGQDs are uniformly dispersed across the lamellar surface of CaIn₂S₄ [46,47]. It may be attributed to the growth of NGQDs hindering the accumulation of CaIn2S4 nanosheets, thereby resulting in the exhibition of a greater number of thin-layer sheet structures [48]. Concurrently, CaIn2S4 serves as a structural directing agent that prevents the accumulation of NGQDs. The synergistic effect of both components enhances the specific surface area of CaIS/NGQDs-2. The thin-layer structure favors the full exposure of catalytic active sites and promotes charge migration between Sv-CaIn2S4 nanosheets and NGQDs [49]. Fig. 5(d) presents a magnified view of Fig. 5(c), specifically showing the HRTEM image of CaIS/NGQDs-2. The lattice spacing of 0.24 nm corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [50], while the lattice spacing of 0.27 nm matches the (400) plane of CaIn2S4 [51]. Both SEM and TEM data confirm the successful loading of NGQDs onto CaIn2S4.

The surface area and pore volume of CaIn2S4, CaIS/NGQDs-1, CaIS/NGQDs-2, and CaIS/NGQDs-3 were investigated via BET testing. Fig. 6(a) shows that these samples display a mesoporous structure typical of type IV isotherms, accompanied by H3-type hysteresis loops in the P/P0 range from 0.4 to 1.0 [52]. The calculated surface areas for CaIn2S4 and CaIS/NGQDs-X (X = 1, 2, 3) are 32.3 m2/g, 54.0 m2/g, 225.8 m2/g, and 78.7 m2/g, respectively. In Fig. 6(b), the pore volumes of CaIn2S4 and CaIS/NGQDs-X (X = 1, 2, 3) are 0.06 cm3/g, 0.17 cm3/g, 0.43 cm3/g, and 0.16 cm3/g, respectively. Among the CaIS/NGQDs composites, CaIS/NGQDs-2 has the largest surface area and pore volume, which are 7.0 times and 7.2 times those of CaIn2S4, respectively. The larger surface area and pore volume of CaIS/NGQDs-2 can be attributed to the loaded NGQDs and the thin-layer structure of CaIn2S4. The increase in the contact area between the photocatalyst and pollutants enhances the adsorption capacity of the photocatalyst and provides more active sites for photocatalytic reactions, thereby promoting the overall effect of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants [53].

Fig. 7(a) presents the UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) of CaIn2S4, CaIS/NGQDs-1, CaIS/NGQDs-2, and CaIS/NGQDs-3. After the loading of NGQDs, the absorption edge of CaIS/NGQDs composites gradually shows a blue shift, which can be attributed to the quantum size effect [54]. Correspondingly, Fig. 7(b) shows the determination of the bandgap width of the samples using the Kubelka-Munk function. The bandgap widths of CaIS/NGQDs-1 (2.13 eV), CaIS/NGQDs-2 (2.39 eV), and CaIS/NGQDs-3 (2.40 eV) are wider than that of CaIn2S4 (2.06 eV) [55]. Based on the VB-XPS spectra in Fig. 7(c), the valence band (VB) potentials of CaIn2S4 and CaIS/NGQDs-X (X = 1, 2, 3) are determined to be 1.31 eV, 1.13 eV, 0.86 eV, and 0.56 eV, respectively [56]. By combining the bandgap width and the valence band position, the conduction band (CB) potentials of CaIn2S4 and CaIS/NGQDs-X (X = 1, 2, 3) are calculated to be −0.75 eV, −1.00 eV, −1.53 eV, and −1.84 eV, respectively. An energy level diagram is plotted in Fig. 7(d) [57]. The band information indicates that band structure of CaIn2S4 was modified by the loading of NGQDs, and resulting in a more negative conduction band position for CaIS/NGQDs composites. This enables free electrons to exhibit stronger reducing capabilities, converting O2 into ·O2 - [58].

The photocurrent density measured via photoelectrochemical (PEC) analysis shows that CaIS/NGQDs-2 exhibits a significantly higher photocurrent density, approximately 20 times that of CaIn2S4, as shown in Fig. 8(a). This observation implies that the photogenerated carriers in the pure phase CaIn2S4 are prone to recombination, while the carriers separation and transfer capabilities of CaIS/NGQDs-2 are significantly improved [59]. To further confirm the improved carrier transport efficiency of CaIS/NGQDs-2, EIS was carried out [60]. As shown in Fig. 8(b), both CaIn2S4, CaIS/NGQDs-1, CaIS/NGQDs-2, and CaIS/NGQDs-3 impedance curves conform to the Nyquist model. Notably, compared with CaIn2S4, the CaIS/NGQDs composites exhibited a smaller arc radius, with CaIS/NGQDs-2 having the smallest arc radius. This indicates that CaIS/NGQDs-2 has the fastest charge transfer rate and the highest carrier separation rate [61]. Fig. 8(c) displays the steady-state PL spectra of CaIn2S4, CaIS/NGQDs-1, CaIS/NGQDs-2, and CaIS/NGQDs-3. After the loading of NGQDs, the PL signal intensity of the CaIS/NGQDs composites decreased, indicating that the loading of NGQDs significantly reduced the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs [62]. Among them, the CaIS/NGQDs-2 sample exhibited the lowest peak intensity, suggesting that an appropriate amount of NGQDs loading facilitated the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers in the system, significantly enhancing the photocatalytic performance of CaIS/NGQDs-2 [63]. Fig. 8(d) presents the PL spectrum of NGQDs, with an optimal excitation wavelength of 440 nm. The fluorescence emission spectrum of NGQDs exhibits excitation-dependent behavior, where the emission peak wavelength gradually increases as the excitation wavelength increases from 380 nm to 480 nm [64,65].

Using a Xenon lamp to simulate solar light, the photocatalytic performance of CaIn2S4, CaIS/NGQDs-1, CaIS/NGQDs-2, and CaIS/NGQDs-3 was compared for the degradation of RhB ( Fig. 9(a)). After 30 minutes of dark adsorption, the removal rate of RhB reached 30.5 % for CaIn2S4, while it increased to 54.6 % for CaIS/NGQDs-2 loaded with NGQDs. The enhanced adsorption capacity is primarily attributed to the formation of π-π stacking between the aromatic rings in NGQDs and the RhB molecules containing aromatic ring structures, which strengthens the adsorption performance of CaIS/NGQDs-2 [66]. After 10 minutes of illumination, the removal rate of RhB significantly increased to 96.4 % for CaIS/NGQDs-2, significantly higher than the 47.3 % removal rate of CaIn2S4. After 40 minutes of illumination, the removal rate of RhB by CaIS/NGQDs-2 reached 99.8 %, which was higher than that of CaIn2S4 (69.9 %), CaIS/NGQDs-1 (77.9 %), and CaIS/NGQDs-3 (99.0 %). To further investigate the photocatalytic performance of the samples, degradation kinetic curves were drawn, as shown in the inset of Fig. 9(a) [67]. The reaction kinetic constants were calculated using the first-order degradation kinetic formula, resulting in degradation rate constants of −0.0202, −0.0260, −0.1254, and −0.1001 min−1 for CaIn2S4 and CaIS/NGQDs-X (X = 1, 2, 3), respectively. Faster degradation rates of CaIS/NGQDs-X (X = 1, 2, 3) are due to loading of NGQDs. Among them, CaIS/NGQDs-2 shows the most significant enhancement, approximately 6.2 times faster than pure-phase CaIn2S4. The substantially improved degradation rate is attributed to the stacked thin-layer structure of CaIS/NGQDs-2 after the loading of NGQDs, which results in a larger specific surface area and more porous structures to provide more active sites for the sample. Additionally, the loading of NGQDs enhances the conductivity of CaIS/NGQDs-2 to facilitate charge transport. Furthermore, as an electron transfer center, NGQDs can suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Moreover, Fig. 9(b) indicates that CaIS/NGQDs-2 also exhibits excellent degradation capabilities for other pollutants. After 40 minutes of irradiation, the CaIS/NGQDs-2 composite (10 mg) achieved degradation efficiencies of 99.5 % for congo red (CR), 93.9 % for methyl orange (MO), and 99.7 % for methylene blue (MB). For pharmaceutical contaminants, the sample reached 98.3 % degradation of tetracycline (TC) and 97.9 % degradation of levofloxacin (LEV).

The stability of the samples in degrading RhB was tested through cycling experiments, as shown in Fig. 9(c) [68]. The sample exhibits stable catalytic performance. After five cycles, the photocatalytic activity of CaIS/NGQDs-2 slightly decreased but still maintained an efficiency of over 90 %, indicating that the sample has stable catalytic performance. To investigate the contribution of various active species in photocatalysis, scavengers were introduced to capture reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated during the degradation process, specifically superoxide radicals (·O2 -), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), and holes (h+). The scavengers used were p-benzoquinone (PBQ), tert-butanol (TBA), and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), respectively, as shown in Fig. 9(d) [69]. During the photocatalytic reaction, the decrease in photocatalytic activity is proportional to the extent of participation of the respective radicals in the reaction. Upon the addition of scavengers, the degradation efficiency of CaIS/NGQDs-2 decreased from its initial value of 99.8 % to 72.1 % (PBQ), 94.8 % (TBA), and 84.4 % (EDTA-2Na), respectively. Based on the inhibitory effects of the three scavengers on the degradation of CaIS/NGQDs-2, it is evident that ·O2 - is the primary active species responsible for the efficient degradation of RhB by CaIS/NGQDs-2, followed by h+. This observation aligns with the band structure diagrams of CaIn2S4 and CaIS/NGQDs-2 (Fig. 7(d)). Before and after modification, the CB potentials of CaIn2S4 and CaIS/NGQDs-2 are −0.75 eV and −1.53 eV, respectively, both negative to the standard redox potential of O2/·O2 - (-0.33 eV vs Normal Hydrogen Electrode (NHE)) [70]. It indicates that both CaIn2S4 and CaIS/NGQDs-2 has a ability to reduce O2 to ·O2 -. On the contrary, the VB potentials of CaIn2S4 and CaIS/NGQDs-2 are 1.31 eV and 0.86 eV, respectively, both higher than the standard oxidation potential of ·OH/OH- (1.99 eV vs NHE), thus OH- cannot be oxidized to ·OH. As a result, ·O2 - is the primary active species responsible for the degradation process in both CaIn2S4 and CaIS/NGQDs-2.

Based on the aforementioned analysis, a photocatalytic enhancement mechanism of CaIS/NGQDs-2 under simulated solar light irradiation is proposed, and a schematic diagram of the photocatalytic mechanism is depicted in Fig. 10. The lamellar structure of CaIn2S4 loaded NGQDs enhance the contact area between CaIS/NGQDs-2 and pollutants, thereby improving the adsorption capacity of the photocatalyst and ultimately enhancing the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. Upon irradiation, electrons and holes generated in the CB and VB of CaIn2S4, respectively (Eq. 3) excited electrons on the CB was captured by the Sv and transferred to the NGQDs. The CaIS/NGQDs-2 CB potential of −1.53 eV (vs. NHE) is indeed lower than the potential of O2/·O2 - (-0.33 eV vs. NHE), which enables the photogenerated electrons to reduce O2 to ·O2 - (Eq. 4) [71]. Concurrently, the reactive oxygen species (ROS) can react with RhB and decompose it into intermediate metabolites, CO2, and H2O (Eq. 5) [72].
(3) CaIS/NGQDs-2 + hʋ → e- + h+
(4) e- CB+O2 → ·O2 -
(5) ·O2 -/h+ VB/·OH + RhB → Intermediates metabolites + CO2 + H2O

The superior photocatalytic activity of CaIS/NGQDs-2 can be attributed to the following key factors: (1) The thin-layer structure of CaIS/NGQDs-2 can shorten the diffusion distance of charge carriers. enabling photogenerated electrons and holes to migrate more efficiently to the surface. Moreover, the specific surface area and pore volume of CaIS/NGQDs-2 have increased to 4.2 and 2.5 times that of CaIn2S4, respectively, which provide more reactive sites for photocatalytic reactions. (2) The Sv serve as electron traps, capable of capturing the excited electrons on CaIn2S4 after illumination, thereby enhancing the charge migration efficiency and effectively separating the photogenerated carriers, leading to the prolongation of the carrier recombination time. The prolonged the recombination time of the carriers can be verified by reduced PL intensity. Additionally, the Sv can serve as active sites to accelerate the consumption of electrons. (3) As electron transport centers, NGQDs transmit electrons to NGQDs as electron acceptors to promote the directional transfer of photogenerated charges, thus the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes were hindered. Due to the excellent conductivity of NGQDs, the electrical conductivity of CaIS/NGQDs-2 were significantly enhanced by NGQDs loaded, to facilitate the more charges transfering. Compared with CaIn2S4, approximately 20 fold increase in the photocurrent density of CaIS/NGQDs-2, as well as a decrease in electrochemical impedance and PL intensity can be attributed to the increase in the number of Sv and NGQDs as electron transfer centers with enhanced conductivity. The aforementioned synergistic effects not only allow more photogenerated charges to participate in redox reactions in CaIS/NGQDs-2, but also optimize the transfer channels of photogenerated charges, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst.

In summary, this experiment successfully synthesized the CaIS/NGQDs composite photocatalysts with excellent stability and photocatalytic performance through a two-step hydrothermal method. Among them, CaIS/NGQDs-2 exhibited the highest photocatalytic degradation performance, achieving nearly complete degradation of RhB with a degradation rate of up to 99.8 % after irradiation for 40 minutes under a simulated solar light source of a 300 W Xenon lamp. The study found that, CaIS/NGQDs-2 significantly increases the specific surface area and pore volume of the sample to facilitate contact with RhB and increase the number of active sites. More importantly, thin-layer structure of CaIS/NGQDs-2 effectively shortened the migration distance of electrons from the interior to the surface of CaIn2S4. Furthermore, the abundant Sv not only served as electron traps to effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes but also provided abundant surface active sites for catalytic reactions. On the one hand, the introduction of NGQDs as electron acceptors improved the electrical conductivity to constructed directional electron transfer channels, on the other hand, the electron transfer ability between Sv-CaIn2S4 and NGQDs was enhanced to suppresses the recombination of photo generated electron hole pairs. This work demonstrates the capability of carbon material modification and vacancy engineering to enhance the degradation efficiency of CaIn2S4, and proposes the growth mode between CaIn2S4 and NGQDs as well as the photocatalytic mechanism of action.
